origin: A fragment of a biblical genealogy in Ancient Greek, specifically matching the sequence “Aram/Aram begot Aminadab; Aminadab begot Nahshon; Nahshon begot Salmon,” which appears in the genealogy of Jesus in the Gospel of Matthew (Matt. 1:4–5). The line reads like a straight transcription of ancestral listings used for theological/identity purposes.
author: Most likely the author/collector of the Gospel of Matthew (i.e., the Matthean evangelist or his textual tradition). In practice, the immediate “authorial” voice is that of a compiler copying/arranging genealogical material rather than a personal witness.
thoughts: The author’s perspective is fundamentally religious and documentary: the motivation is to establish lineage through authoritative ancestral names, linking present/future religious claims (e.g., messianic identity) to scriptural genealogies. There’s little emotional stance in the line itself—more a sense of “recording the proper succession,” trusting the list as legitimate tradition.
audience: Greek-speaking Jewish or early Christian readers familiar with Hebrew/Scripture-based genealogies, who would understand the significance of connecting family lines to covenant history and messianic expectations.
written_when: Likely in the 1st century CE (commonly dated to the 70–90 CE window for Matthew), when Greek-language Christian texts were being composed for diaspora communities.
